source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure that Dataproc Cluster is encrypted using Customer- Managed Encryption Key

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure that Dataproc Cluster is encrypted using Customer- Managed Encryption Key
input_row.description: When you use Dataproc, cluster and job data is stored on Persistent Disks (PDs) associated with the Compute Engine VMs in your cluster and in a Cloud Storage staging bucket. This PD and bucket data is encrypted using a Google-generated data encryption key (DEK) and key encryption key (KEK). The CMEK feature allows you to create, use, and revoke the key encryption key (KEK). Google still controls the data encryption key (DEK).
input_row.rationale: "Cloud services offer the ability to protect data related to those services using encryption keys managed by the customer within Cloud KMS. These encryption keys are called customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK). When you protect data in Google Cloud services with CMEK, the CMEK key is within your control. Impact: Using Customer Managed Keys involves additional overhead in maintenance by administrators.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Login to the GCP Console and navigate to the Dataproc Cluster page by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/dataproc/clusters. 2. Select the project from the project dropdown list. 3. On the Dataproc Clusters page, select the cluster and click on the Name attribute value that you want to examine. 4. On the details page, select the Configurations tab. 5. On the Configurations tab, check the Encryption type configuration attribute value. If the value is set to Google-managed key, then Dataproc Cluster is not encrypted with Customer managed encryption keys. Repeat step no. 3 - 5 for other Dataproc Clusters available in the selected project. 6. Change the project from the project dropdown list and repeat the audit procedure for other projects. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Run clusters list command to list all the Dataproc Clusters available in the region: gcloud dataproc clusters list --region='us-central1' 2. Run clusters describe command to get the key details of the selected cluster: gcloud dataproc clusters describe <cluster_name> --region=us-central1 -- flatten=config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName 3. If the above command output return "null", then the selected cluster is not encrypted with Customer managed encryption keys. 4. Repeat step no. 2 and 3 for other Dataproc Clusters available in the selected region. Change the region by updating --region and repeat step no. 2 for other clusters available in the project. Change the project by running the below command and repeat the audit procedure for other Dataproc clusters available in other projects: gcloud config set project <project_ID>"
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Login to the GCP Console and navigate to the Dataproc Cluster page by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/dataproc/clusters. 2. Select the project from the projects dropdown list. 3. On the Dataproc Cluster page, click on the Create Cluster to create a new cluster with Customer managed encryption keys. 4. On Create a cluster page, perform below steps: • Inside Set up cluster section perform below steps: -In the Name textbox, provide a name for your cluster. o From Location select the location in which you want to deploy a cluster. o Configure other configurations as per your requirements. • Inside Configure Nodes and Customize cluster section configure the settings as per your requirements. • Inside Manage security section, perform below steps: o From Encryption, select Customer-managed key. o Select a customer-managed key from dropdown list. o Ensure that the selected KMS Key have Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter role assign to Dataproc Cluster service account ("serviceAccount:service-<project_number>@compute- system.iam.gserviceaccount.com"). o Click on Create to create a cluster. • Once the cluster is created migrate all your workloads from the older cluster to the new cluster and delete the old cluster by performing the below steps: o On the Clusters page, select the old cluster and click on Delete cluster. o On the Confirm deletion window, click on Confirm to delete the cluster. o Repeat step above for other Dataproc clusters available in the selected project. • Change the project from the project dropdown list and repeat the remediation procedure for other Dataproc clusters available in other projects. From Google Cloud CLI Before creating cluster ensure that the selected KMS Key have Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter role assign to Dataproc Cluster service account ("serviceAccount:service-<project_number>@compute- system.iam.gserviceaccount.com"). Run clusters create command to create new cluster with customer-managed key: gcloud dataproc clusters create <cluster_name> --region=us-central1 --gce-pd- kms-key=<key_resource_name> The above command will create a new cluster in the selected region. Once the cluster is created migrate all your workloads from the older cluster to the new cluster and Run clusters delete command to delete cluster: gcloud dataproc clusters delete <cluster_name> --region=us-central1 Repeat step no. 1 to create a new Dataproc cluster. Change the project by running the below command and repeat the remediation procedure for other projects: gcloud config set project <project_ID>" References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/docs/security/encryption/default-encryption
input_row.impact: Using Customer Managed Keys involves additional overhead in maintenance by administrators.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

After reviewing all fields—`description`, `rationale`, `audit`, `remediation`, `impact`, and `references`—it is determined that this control can be audited through automation. This decision leverages:

- The `audit` field already provides a detailed process using Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) CLI commands to verify if a Dataproc Cluster is using a Customer-Managed Encryption Key (CMEK).
- The 'gcloud dataproc clusters describe' command is explicitly mentioned to check the 'encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName' property, which can determine whether CMEK is used.

The use of GCP services, such as Dataproc alongside GCP APIs, validates that the property is accessible programmatically. This encryption check is a standard GCP service property and does not require human judgment or visual inspection.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_dataproc_cluster_cmek_encrypted

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

This automated program will perform the following tasks:

1. **Enumerate Dataproc Clusters:**
   - Use `gcloud dataproc clusters list --region=<region>` to list all Dataproc clusters in the specified region.

2. **Check CMEK Configuration:**
   - For each cluster, execute `gcloud dataproc clusters describe <cluster_name> --region=<region> --flatten=config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName`.
   - Verify that `config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName` is not `null`.

3. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass:** If `config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName` is set (not `null`), indicating the cluster is encrypted with a Customer-managed key.
   - **Fail:** If `config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName` is `null`, suggesting the cluster is not encrypted with a CMEK and uses the default Google-managed key instead.

This approach ensures comprehensive verification of Dataproc cluster encryption using Customer-managed keys following CIS GCP benchmark guidelines.
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. The presence of CMEK configuration for Dataproc clusters is fully discoverable via GCP APIs and the `gcloud` CLI. No subjective human review is required.
- **GCP SDK Capabilities:**  
  The `gcloud dataproc clusters describe` command exposes the relevant field (`config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName`). This can be scripted and integrated into automated compliance pipelines.
- **Other Considerations:**  
  While GCP Config (now called "Asset Inventory" or "Cloud Asset Inventory") could also be used for continuous monitoring, the CLI/API approach is valid and sufficient for audit purposes.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 9/10**

- **Automation Details:**  
  - The steps to enumerate clusters and check the CMEK field are technically accurate.
  - The `gcloud dataproc clusters list` and `gcloud dataproc clusters describe` commands are correct.
  - The use of `--flatten=config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName` is appropriate for extracting the CMEK property.
- **Program Name:**  
  - The program name `gcp_dataproc_cluster_cmek_encrypted` is clear but could be more descriptive (see Improvements).
- **Completeness:**  
  - The check only covers CMEK for Persistent Disks (PDs) but does not address the Cloud Storage staging bucket, which is also mentioned in the CIS control description. The audit should clarify whether CMEK is also required for the staging bucket and, if so, check the bucket's encryption configuration as well.

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Cloud Storage Staging Bucket:**  
  - The CIS control description references both PDs and the Cloud Storage staging bucket. The current automation only checks the PDs. For completeness and strict CIS alignment, the automation should also:
    - Identify the staging bucket used by each Dataproc cluster (available in the cluster configuration).
    - Check the bucket's encryption configuration using `gsutil bucket get` or `gcloud storage buckets describe <bucket>`, and verify if a CMEK is set (`encryption.defaultKmsKeyName`).
- **Alternative GCP API Methods:**  
  - The same checks can be performed via the REST API (`dataproc.projects.regions.clusters.get`) or using client libraries for more scalable automation.
- **Program Name Suggestion:**  
  - To be more descriptive, consider:  
    `gcp_dataproc_cluster_pd_and_bucket_cmek_enforced`  
    or  
    `gcp_dataproc_cluster_cmek_at_rest_encryption`
- **Output and Reporting:**  
  - Recommend that the automation outputs both the cluster name and the CMEK key used (if any), and flags clusters/buckets missing CMEK.
- **Multi-Region Coverage:**  
  - Ensure the script iterates over all regions, not just a single specified region, unless the scope is intentionally limited.

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The technical approach is sound and aligns with GCP best practices.
- Minor improvement needed for full coverage of the CIS control (Cloud Storage bucket CMEK).

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **MODIFY**

- **Action:**  
  - Update the automation to also check the CMEK configuration of the Cloud Storage staging bucket associated with each Dataproc cluster.
  - Optionally, update the program name to reflect the full scope of the check (PDs and buckets, or "at rest" encryption).
- **Rationale:**  
  - This ensures full alignment with the CIS benchmark's intent and provides a comprehensive compliance assessment.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section              | Assessment         | Notes                                                                                  |
|----------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation  | AGREE             | Automated is correct; gcloud/API fully support this check.                             |
| Technical Accuracy   | 9/10              | Accurate for PDs, but missing Cloud Storage bucket CMEK check.                         |
| Improvements         | See above         | Add bucket CMEK check, clarify scope, improve program name.                            |
| Confidence           | HIGH              | Only minor gaps; core logic is solid.                                                  |
| Final Recommendation | MODIFY            | Add Cloud Storage CMEK check for full CIS compliance; optionally update program name.  |

---
